title: Public Safety Data Amendments
short_description: This bill amends provisions related to public safety data.
detailed_description: This bill:<hr><ltbullet>removes a report required to be submitted by the Bureau of Criminal Identification to the Law Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee regarding attempted weapons purchases by individuals who are restricted from purchasing, possessing, or transferring a firearm as a result of a conviction for an offense involving domestic violence and requires the report to be submitted to the State Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice (commission) to be included in the public safety portal;<hr><ltbullet>removes a report required to be submitted by the Division of Juvenile Justice and Youth Services to the Law Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee regarding data of sexual assaults in detention and secure care facilities and requires the report to be submitted to the commission to be included in the public safety portal;<hr><ltbullet>removes a report required to be submitted by the Department of Corrections to the Law Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee regarding data of sexual assaults in correctional facilities and requires the report to be submitted to the commission to be included in the public safety portal;<hr><ltbullet>requires institutions of higher education to provide data of crime in student housing to the commission to be included in the public safety portal;<hr><ltbullet> requires the multi-agency strike force to combat violent and other major felony crimes to submit the strike force's annual report to the commission to be included in the public safety portal;<hr><ltbullet>requires the commission to include a report from the Board of Pardons and Parole regarding metrics of offenders in the public safety portal;<hr><ltbullet>requires the Prosecutor Conduct Commission to provide the Prosecutor Conduct Commission's annual report on prosecutorial complaints to the commission to be included in the public safety portal;<hr><ltbullet>requires the Department of Public Safety and the Bureau of Forensic Services to provide an annual report on sexual assault kits to the commission to be included in the public safety portal;<hr><ltbullet>adds requirements for the commission regarding the public safety portal;<hr><ltbullet>clarifies that the commission is not required to provide records in response to a record request under the Government Records Access and Management Act for records received by the commission for the public safety portal; and<hr><ltbullet>makes technical can conforming changes.

title: Dyed Diesel Fuel Search Amendments
short_description: This bill addresses searching a vehicle for a dyed diesel fuel violation.
detailed_description: This bill:<hr><ltbullet>establishes that a government official may only inspect a vehicle for a dyed diesel fuel violation if the official:<hr><ltbullet1>has probable cause that a dyed diesel fuel violation has occurred;<hr><ltbullet1>has consent of the owner of the vehicle; or<hr><ltbullet1>is conducting the search at a port-of-entry in compliance with Section 72-9-501; and<hr><ltbullet>specifies that probable cause is not established simply by observing an individual drive a motor vehicle that uses diesel fuel on a highway.

title: Candidate Petition Amendments
short_description: This bill amends provisions related to candidate nomination petitions.
detailed_description: This bill:<hr><ltbullet>requires a candidate who seeks the nomination for an elective office by gathering signatures of registered voters to submit the signatures to the county clerk of each registered voter's residence;<hr><ltbullet>provides that a county clerk may only certify the signature of a registered voter described above if the registered voter is a resident of the county clerk's county;<hr><ltbullet>for a signature gathering candidate who seeks the nomination for an office in a district where the officeholder is elected by the voters from more than one county, requires the county clerk of each county to which the candidate submits signatures to:<hr><ltbullet1>count the number of valid signatures submitted by the candidate; and<hr><ltbullet1>certify the number of valid signatures to the lieutenant governor; <hr><ltbullet> for an elective office described above, requires the lieutenant governor to:<hr><ltbullet1>count the total number of valid signatures received from each county clerk; and<hr><ltbullet1>if the aggregate number of valid signatures is sufficient for the candidate to qualify for placement on the ballot, certify the signatures;<hr><ltbullet>amends the candidate nomination petition forms to specify that only a registered voter residing in the county where a candidate submits signatures for verification should sign the candidate's nomination petition;<hr><ltbullet>for a candidate for elective office seeking the nomination of a qualified political party through signature-gathering, shortens the deadline for the candidate to submit signatures to an election official from 14 to 21 days before the day on which the party holds a convention to nominate a candidate for the same elective office; and<hr><ltbullet>makes technical and conforming changes.

title: Signature Verification Revisions
short_description: This bill amends provisions related to candidate nomination petitions.
detailed_description: This bill:<hr><ltbullet>requires an election official to post the name, voter precinct number, and date of signature of a registered voter who signs a candidate nomination petition on the lieutenant governor's website for at least 90 days after the date of an election;<hr><ltbullet>requires an election official to send an email notice to a registered voter described above informing the voter that:<hr><ltbullet1>the voter's name and voter-related information is posted on the lieutenant governor's website; and<hr><ltbullet1>the voter may request to have the voter's signature removed from the candidate nomination petition by submitting a written statement to the election official requesting removal of the signature;<hr><ltbullet>requires an election official to make the website posting described above regardless of whether the voter's voter registration record is classified as a private record;<hr><ltbullet>amends candidate nomination petition forms to include information notifying a voter of the posting and signature removal processes described above; <hr><ltbullet>for a candidate seeking the nomination of a qualified political party to an elective office through signature gathering, shortens the deadline for the candidate to submit signatures to an election official from 14 to 21 days before the day on which the qualified political party holds a convention to nominate a candidate for the same elective office; and<hr><ltbullet>makes technical and conforming changes.

title: Signature Gathering and Verification Amendments
short_description: This bill modifies requirements for petitions.
detailed_description: This bill:<hr><ltbullet>amends, standardizes, and makes technical revisions to the signature collector verification form included in petition packets;<hr><ltbullet>specifies how an individual may be paid for collecting petition signatures;<hr><ltbullet>beginning on July 1, 2027, requires a clerk who verifies signatures for a petition to send an email and text message notice to the signer of the petition notifying the signer of the status of the signer's signature;<hr><ltbullet>requires the lieutenant governor to develop and maintain an online training course to educate individuals who collect signatures for a petition;<hr><ltbullet>requires an individual who collects signatures for a petition to complete the training course described above before collecting any signatures for the petition;<hr><ltbullet>requires an election official to reject a signature packet if the individual who collects signatures for the petition did not complete the online training course described above;<hr><ltbullet>modifies requirements related to statewide initiative and referendum packets by:<hr><ltbullet1>removing the requirement that the county clerk deliver each packet to the lieutenant governor; <hr><ltbullet1>requiring the county clerk who verifies packets to certify certain statistical information to the lieutenant governor; and<hr><ltbullet1>directing the county clerk to retain and preserve each packet for at least 22 months;<hr><ltbullet>designates the existing initiative and referendum packet format as one packet type and creates an alternative packet type with different content and assembly requirements;<hr><ltbullet>requires an alternative packet type to provide access to the text of a proposed or referable law through a QR code rather than a printed copy;<hr><ltbullet>for an alternative packet type, requires the initiative petition and each signature sheet to contain a brief, plain-language description of the principal provisions of the law proposed by the initiative;<hr><ltbullet>allows the sponsors of an initiative or referendum petition to circulate packets using a traditional packet type, an alternative packet type, or both;<hr><ltbullet>repeals provisions permitting an individual to optionally provide the individual's email address when signing an initiative or referendum signature sheet;<hr><ltbullet>updates the formatting and spacing requirements for initiative and referendum signature sheets;<hr><ltbullet>prohibits the sponsors of an initiative or referendum petition from marking or redacting a signature sheet in a manner that obscures, conceals, or renders illegible a signer's date of signature or other voter information used for verification;<hr><ltbullet>provides that a county clerk may not certify a signature if the date of signature is later than the applicable deadline for submitting an initiative or referendum packet;<hr><ltbullet>on January 1, 2027, repeals provisions requiring the sponsors of an initiative petition to:<hr><ltbullet1>send an informational email to each initiative petition signer who provides a legible email address on a signature sheet; and<hr><ltbullet1>send a list to the lieutenant governor identifying the recipients of the email described above;<hr><ltbullet>beginning on January 1, 2027, requires an election officer to send the email described above to each eligible voter who signs an initiative petition within two business days of verifying the voter's signature;<hr><ltbullet>makes technical revisions to the required statements that appear on initiative and referendum signature sheets;<hr><ltbullet>modifies certain statements appearing on initiative and referendum packets to provide that:<hr><ltbullet1>the signer of the packet had an opportunity to read and understand the proposed or referable law; and<hr><ltbullet1>for the signature gatherer, the signature gatherer believes that the signer had an opportunity to read and understand the proposed or referable law; and<hr><ltbullet>makes technical and conforming changes.

title: Political Signs Amendments
short_description: This bill amends provisions related to political signs.
detailed_description: This bill:<hr><ltbullet>defines terms;<hr><ltbullet>specifies the form and placement of the disclosure statement for political advertisements;<hr><ltbullet>creates an exception to political advertising disclosure requirements for certain candidate-paid political signs; <hr><ltbullet>reduces the size dimensions of a political sign that is exempt from disclosing, on the sign, the person who paid for the sign;<hr><ltbullet>prohibits a person from:<hr><ltbullet1>attaching a political sign to a utility pole, light pole, an electric utility box, a traffic control device, or any railroad sign or signal; or<hr><ltbullet1>affixing an object to a political sign for the purpose of blocking, concealing, or misrepresenting the message or image on the political sign;<hr><ltbullet>expands existing criminal penalties for defacing political signs to cover the conduct described above, and specifies exceptions for:<hr><ltbullet1>a person who removes a political sign that does not contain the disclosure described above;<hr><ltbullet1>a person who removes a political sign that violates the prohibition on electioneering in or near a polling place;<hr><ltbullet1>a government employee who removes a political sign in the course of the government employee's duties;<hr><ltbullet1>a person who removes an object that blocks or conceals a political sign; or<hr><ltbullet1>a person who removes a political sign that is attached to a utility pole, light pole, an electric utility box, a traffic control device, or any railroad sign or signal;<hr><ltbullet>requires each county and municipality to designate one or more locations where a person who removes a political sign may deposit the political sign for safekeeping or disposal;<hr><ltbullet>requires a person who removes a political sign under specified circumstances to deposit the sign at a designated location described above;<hr><ltbullet>for a county or municipality that receives a political sign, requires the county or municipality to:<hr><ltbullet1>contact the owner of the political sign; and<hr><ltbullet1>notify the owner to take possession of the political sign;<hr><ltbullet>authorizes a county or municipality to, in certain circumstances:<hr><ltbullet1>dispose of a political sign; and<hr><ltbullet1>seek reimbursement from the owner of the political sign for the sign's safekeeping or disposal;<hr><ltbullet>prohibits a county or municipality from prohibiting a property owner or lawful occupant of property adjacent to a park strip from posting a political sign on the park strip; and<hr><ltbullet>makes technical and conforming changes.

title: Local Government Amendments
short_description: This bill deals with provisions related to counties and special districts.
detailed_description: This bill:<hr><ltbullet>defines the term "finance officer" and modifies definitions;<hr><ltbullet>describes when a county legislative body shall submit the question of moving the county seat to the county's voters;<hr><ltbullet>modifies provisions related to the annexation or consolidation of counties and the creation of a new county;<hr><ltbullet>addresses pending criminal and civil proceedings following the creation of a new county;<hr><ltbullet>provides that a county shall hold an election on an optional plan to change the form of county government at the next regular general election that is no less than 180 days after the day on which the county attorney submits to the county clerk the attorney's report;<hr><ltbullet>modifies provisions related to county costs following a change of venue;<hr><ltbullet>repeals a requirement that a county financial officer be bonded;<hr><ltbullet>modifies the ability of a legislative body to investigate the actions of a county officer that impacts county business or operations;<hr><ltbullet>modifies provisions related to the consolidation of county offices;<hr><ltbullet>modifies the qualification requirements of a county assessor;<hr><ltbullet>provides that, if a county council delegates the provision of accounting services to a finance officer, the county auditor is not required to fulfill an accounting obligation described in statute unless statute explicitly states that the county auditor shall provide the accounting obligation;<hr><ltbullet>modifies provisions related to county surveyor duties;<hr><ltbullet>modifies the circumstances in which an election official is required to notify eligible voters via email about the disqualification of a candidate;<hr><ltbullet>modifies the manner in which a county recorder is required to index recorded instruments;<hr><ltbullet>authorizes a county legislative body to set fees for services of the county recorder;<hr><ltbullet>increases certain statutorily provided fees for certain county recorder services;<hr><ltbullet>provides a method for an individual requesting a record related to military service to demonstrate that the individual is a lineal descendant of the individual who is the subject of the record;<hr><ltbullet>provides that, when a county contracts with a licensed professional land surveyor to fulfill certain county surveyor duties, the licensed professional land surveyor may utilize the county surveyor seal or a personal seal;<hr><ltbullet>modifies provisions related to survey monuments, corners, and boundaries;<hr><ltbullet>authorizes a county surveyor to charge a plat fee to an infrastructure financing district or public infrastructure district;<hr><ltbullet>authorizes a county treasurer to enter an agreement with a special district for the county treasurer to bill and collect assessments on behalf of the special district;<hr><ltbullet>modifies provisions related to merit system employment, including administrative appeals to a career service council or an administrative law judge;<hr><ltbullet>repeals a criminal penalty for individuals who willfully violate provisions related to county personnel management;<hr><ltbullet>renumbers authority for a county to expend county funds as considered advisable for the development of the county's resources;<hr><ltbullet>repeals sections related to the Title 17 recodification during the 2025 First Special Session;<hr><ltbullet>recodifies Title 17B, Chapter 2a, Part 13, Infrastructure Financing District, as Title 17D, Chapter 5, Infrastructure Financing District Act; and<hr><ltbullet>makes technical and conforming changes.

and<hr><ltbullet1>the Department of Corrections regarding supervision models used in state correctional facilities;<hr><ltbullet>removes the requirement for a report submitted to the Law Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee and the Political Subdivisions Interim Committee from the Division of Emergency Management regarding the emergency alert system throughout the state;<hr><ltbullet>removes the requirement for a report submitted to the Judiciary Interim Committee, the Health and Human Services Interim Committee, the Law Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee, and related appropriations subcommittees from the Department of Corrections regarding certain information about Department of Corrections programs;<hr><ltbullet>removes the requirement for an annual report submitted to the Law Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee, Utah Substance Use and Mental Health Advisory Committee, and an advocacy agency designated by the governor from the State Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice regarding certain statistics gathered from county jails;<hr><ltbullet>removes the requirement for an annual report submitted to the Law Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee and the Utah Substance Use and Mental Health Advisory Committee from the State Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice regarding certain statistics gathered from the Department of Corrections; <hr><ltbullet1>Section 53-28-403, regarding an annual report on crime in student housing;<hr><ltbullet1>Subsection 64-13-6(3)(b), regarding a report submitted by the Department of Corrections to the Law Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee on inmate programs;<hr><ltbullet1>Subsection 78A-2-109.5(6), regarding an annual report by the multi-agency strike force to combat violent and other major felony crimes associated with illegal immigration and human trafficking; short_description: This joint resolution encourages Congress to support policies or programs that assist with successful inmate reentry and enhance security within correctional facilities.

title: Proposal to Amend Utah Constitution - Judicial Nominations
short_description: This joint resolution of the Utah Legislature proposes to amend the Utah Constitution to modify the judicial nominating process.
detailed_description: This resolution:<hr><ltbullet>authorize the Governor to request a list of nominees from a judicial nominating commission when the Governor is filling a judicial vacancy;<hr><ltbullet>authorize the Governor to appoint any qualified candidate even if the candidate is not nominated by the nominating commission; <hr><ltbullet>remove the time constraint on the Governor to make an appointment; and<hr><ltbullet>remove the authority of the chief justice of the Supreme Court to fill a judicial vacancy if the Governor fails to fill the vacancy.
